All right, you're back. Time for the next lesson in body language. It's a great one. Interesting story. So I'm getting interviewed by Victoria Harrison and she's asking me a question about tonality in terms of what do you do if you grew up in the wrong part of town? So you have a guttural slang or you emigrate to a certain country. You're probably saying, wait a second, aren't we in body language? Yeah, we are in body language. Here's the story. So she asked me this question about, you know, how do you solve that problem if you have poor tonality from growing up in the wrong area or you emigrated. And interestingly enough, my answer quickly moves into body language in terms of the interrelation between the two. In other words, you can use body language to trigger certain tonalities and vice versa. So what I want to do now, since we're in the body language module, is I want to show you just how powerful it can be when you harness the power of body language to actually work backwards and trigger certain tonalities. In other words, you're going to have certain tonalities that you struggle with. Most are easy to muster and everybody's different. But once you've identified what those tonalities are that you have trouble with, I'm going to show you now how to actually use body language to make them no brainers for you. So let's cut to that footage right now. I'll see you on the other side. After we're done with this footage, I'm going to actually show you how to go through the exercise yourself and you'll be blown away. I'll see you on the other side. What if you have a really unfortunate thick accent or you come from a specific part of town that's, you know, people don't relate to? That's a great question. And listen, on some level, I won't deny that there are, if you have a thick guttural accent and it happens to be from a place or a part of town that's perceived as not the right one, it's going to work against you. Absolutely. Which is why when I was doing what I was doing at such an intense level, I would actually send people for speech lessons. If their accent was almost detracting from their message, I would teach them to lose their accent. You can drop your accent. Your accent's not something that you're stuck with forever. It's a question of commitment. It's a question of really how bad do you want something? Obviously, let's look at this. Let's just say that it's not so much that it's the accent that's holding you back. But let's say you just have naturally crappy tonality. You're one of those people. What do you think the solution is? Take guess. It's called practice, practice, and practice. You sit there and you look in the mirror and you're underlining things and you keep practicing. That's one element. The second element, which is amazing, is that there's actually a little shortcut to certain tonalities. That is your body language. Typically, with every tonality that you have, there's a body language, a move that you make that essentially will trigger a tonality. Watch. If I want to be certain, I have this thing I do. I go like this. As soon as I'm certain, I always, if I get hyped up and say, believe me, and I will actually smack my hand into the back of my other hand. Now, I can actually do this either way. Either I can get certain in my head first, make my tone certain, and then this follows, or I can actually lead with the movement. I've had situations where I'm training people in a group setting, and some woman cannot, she could not actually lower her voice and tell a secret. I said, tell a secret. Well, Jordan, the reason that you should be like, I don't know, lower your voice. She goes, I'm sorry, Jordan. Well, the reason, say she could not lower her voice. So I said, do me a favor. I want you to narrow your eyes as much as possible and squeeze your fingers together like this. So she's like this, well, Jordan, and as soon as she used the physiological anchor, it triggered the tonality. So what I do, and I suggest what I really want everyone in this training to do here is very important, is I want you actually, if you're having trouble with any tonality at all, any, whether it's certainty, whether it's the reasonable man, whether it's creating scarcity in your voice, whether it's sounding enthusiastic, whether it's the eye care pattern, whether you're having trouble raising questions when they're really declarives there. I want you to actually find a move that you make, your own physiology. Go to a mirror, or actually it's even better, break up with a partner and say, listen, imagine, I want you to, I would say, Victoria, here's a great exercise, Victoria, I want you to pitch me tuna fish sandwiches, and I want you to sound absolutely, look what I just did. I said, absolutely, and immediately I did that movement, absolutely certain about tuna fish. Those tuna fish, and what you'll see is if you start to pitch me, if I say, because it's something ridiculous now, you're not really that certain about tuna fish, but I say, Victoria, sound as certain as you want, what's going to happen is this, you'll lean forward and you'll make some move in an effort to, and then you'll say, oh wait, so then your friends, Victoria, I want you to know that when you sound that certain, you squeeze your right fist and you bang it into your hand. Well, what does that mean? It means that any time in the future, you want to create certainty in your tone, you recreate, and you automatically create, so one can leave the other. So it's incredibly empowering if you're one of those people that's really having trouble creating, mustering those tonalities, then go physiological first, find the move that you make, and you can back into the tonality. All right, you're back pretty cool, right? I'm telling you, listen, I know it sounds a bit wacky, but this works, so here's what I want you to do. Either number one, not as great, but you can do it. I want you to go in the mirror or videotape yourself, and better tape yourself, probably even better, right? And I want you to pick some crazy subject like whale meat or white walls or the sun, say, this whale meat is the best meal for certainty. There's talk about whale meat with absolute certainty. By using that crazy, wacky product, whale meat, right? You can actually talk really certain about something that has no meaning. When you try to apply certainty to something you're certain about, it's when you get locked up. So by choosing that crazy substance, whale meat, that's that crazy, right? But I want you to videotape yourself, and then when you play it back, I want you to watch the body moves that you make, how you move your body, how you trigger yourself to try to create this tonality of absolute certainty. And you'll see that you move your body in a very certain way when you want to create certainty. Conversely, you're struggling with, let's say, telling a secret or scarcity that I want you to actually talk about something like, you know, your favorite baseball team. But my favorite baseball team is in really short supply. See, I can do it for anything, right? But the point is talk about certainty, but with something totally ridiculous, and videotape yourself and watch how you narrow your eyes, how you push your thumbs together in your forefinger. Everybody makes different body language moves that essentially have to muster the proper tonality. You get it? Now, that's one way is videotape. The second way, which I prefer if you're in a company, is break up into groups of two and go through this exercise you have the A's and the B's and the A can be the one with more hair. Remember, it's got more hair, you become the A, remember, it's got less hair, you're the B and you know who you are, right? All right? And what happens is I want you to choose the tonality that you're struggling with and the A's are going to talk about whale meat or whatever, tuna fish salad, right? And you don't talk really certain while the B's observe. After 30 seconds, the B says, okay, let me tell you how you move your body. And that's how you find it out. You get it? And then you go back vice versa. And that's how you can link body language to tonality. Let me just tell you something. I know it might sound strange. Watch how well it works. I've had these experiences with people where again, I said in the interview where a girl could not tell a seat, she could not lower her voice. I said, now you're rise to slits. And I was just like, what did you say? She couldn't help herself. The brain is wired that way. We want to be congruent and we can get there pretty easily once you know how. All right? With that, go mild to your exercises and these are going to be part of your exercises to do it in the real world. Take your quiz and I'll see you in the next lesson.
